definition: Excessively elaborate or showy in style, language, or appearance.
example: The senator's florid rhetoric impressed some listeners but struck others as overwrought and hollow.
mnemonic: FLORID = full of floral excess — too many flowers on the page.

definition: Arrogantly superior and disdainful of others, as if looking down from great height.
example: The haughty duchess refused to acknowledge the servants when she swept through the hall.
mnemonic: HAUGHTY sounds like 'haute' (high French fashion) — someone who thinks they're above everyone.

definition: Overly theatrical or melodramatic in behavior or expression; designed for effect rather than sincerity.
example: Her histrionic outburst at the meeting was so exaggerated that no one took her grievances seriously.
mnemonic: HISTRIONIC shares 'histrio' (Latin actor) — drama queen on stage.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'historical'; histrionic relates to acting, not history.

definition: Assuming power or authority without justification; arrogantly domineering and commanding.
example: The imperious manager barked orders without explanation, expecting instant obedience from the staff.
mnemonic: IMPERIOUS = like an emperor — commanding everyone as if by imperial decree.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'imperial' (of an empire); imperious describes arrogant behavior, not political authority.

definition: Sumptuously rich, elaborate, or extravagant, often to an excessive degree.
example: The lavish banquet featured gold-plated tableware and a ten-course meal for three hundred guests.
mnemonic: LAVISH sounds like 'lav-ish' — washing in wealth, pouring it on.

definition: Restrained in style, size, or ambition; not showy or excessive.
example: The architect's modest design relied on clean lines rather than decorative flourishes.
mnemonic: MODEST = moderate + est — the least flashy option.

definition: Excessive self-admiration and self-centeredness, often accompanied by a lack of empathy for others.
example: His narcissism was evident in the way he steered every conversation back to his own achievements.
mnemonic: NARCISSISM: Narcissus fell in love with his own reflection — total self-obsession.

definition: Asserting authority or offering unsolicited advice in an annoyingly intrusive, self-important manner.
example: The officious clerk insisted on inspecting every document twice, slowing the line to a crawl.
mnemonic: OFFICIOUS: too eager to be the 'official' — poking into everyone's business.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'official'; officious means overbearingly helpful, not holding an office.

definition: Elaborately decorated or adorned, often to a point of excess.
example: The ornate ceiling of the baroque chapel was covered with gilded reliefs and painted cherubs.
mnemonic: ORNATE = ORNAmented to the extreme — more decoration than needed.

definition: Conspicuously displaying wealth or luxury to impress others rather than for genuine pleasure.
example: His ostentatious collection of sports cars seemed designed to provoke envy rather than reflect genuine passion.
mnemonic: OSTENTATIOUS: 'ostent' means to show off — a lavish show put on for spectators.

definition: Flamboyant confidence and stylish verve in manner or performance.
example: She accepted the award with such panache that the audience erupted into spontaneous applause.
mnemonic: PANACHE: a plume (panache in French) on a helmet — worn boldly to show off.

definition: The quality of being excessively self-important or grand in speech and manner.
example: The critic's pomposity alienated younger writers who found his pronouncements insufferably self-congratulatory.
mnemonic: POMPOSITY: a pompous person acts like royalty in a pompous procession.

definition: Behavior adopted for effect or to impress others, with little sincerity behind it.
example: The politician's tough talk on crime was pure posturing ahead of the election, not genuine policy.
mnemonic: POSTURING: striking a pose — like a peacock spreading its feathers for show.

definition: Overstepping proper bounds of behavior through excessive boldness or unwarranted assumption of privilege.
example: It was presumptuous of the intern to rewrite the senior partner's brief without being asked.
mnemonic: PRESUMPTUOUS: PRE-assumes too much — takes liberties before being given permission.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'presumptive'; presumptuous means overly bold, while presumptive means based on reasonable assumption.

definition: Claiming or implying importance, merit, or culture greater than is actually possessed.
example: The pretentious restaurant described its simple pasta dish in three paragraphs of florid prose.
mnemonic: PRETENTIOUS: full of PRETENSE — pretending to be more impressive than you are.

definition: Deliberately exaggerated or shocking to provoke public interest or excitement rather than inform.
example: The tabloid's sensational headlines distorted minor incidents into lurid scandals.
mnemonic: SENSATIONAL: manufactured to create a SENSATION — drama cranked to maximum.
confusableNote: Note the GRE sense (exaggerated/shocking) differs from the colloquial sense (wonderful/excellent).

definition: Settled, sedate, and respectable in character; restrained to the point of dullness.
example: The staid accountancy firm frowned upon any unconventional dress or behavior among its partners.
mnemonic: STAID: stayed still — so settled in old ways that nothing ever changes.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'stayed' (past tense of stay); staid is an adjective meaning overly conservative.

definition: Quiet and restrained in tone or intensity; lacking vividness or forcefulness.
example: The decorator chose a subdued palette of grays and ivories to give the room a calm, understated feel.
mnemonic: SUBDUED: brought under (sub) — toned down, kept beneath full intensity.

definition: Behaving as if one is superior to others; having an air of contemptuous indifference.
example: The professor's supercilious glance at the student's question made the entire class uncomfortable.
mnemonic: SUPERCILIOUS: 'supercilium' is Latin for eyebrow — raising one eyebrow in contemptuous scorn.

definition: Inflated and pompous in style; excessively ornate in language to the point of obscurity.
example: The philosopher's turgid prose buried a modest insight under layers of unnecessary abstraction.
mnemonic: TURGID = swollen up — language puffed up beyond what the content deserves.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'turbid' (murky, unclear liquid); turgid means pompously swollen in style.

definition: Plain and simple, without any decoration or embellishment.
example: The minimalist gallery displayed sculpture on unadorned white pedestals with no labels or commentary.
mnemonic: UNADORNED: UN- + adorn — stripped of all adornment, bare as can be.

definition: Expressed or presented with deliberate restraint, avoiding exaggeration or showiness.
example: Her understated elegance — a simple black dress and single strand of pearls — outshone every elaborate gown in the room.
mnemonic: UNDERSTATED: stated UNDER the full level — less than what could be said, by design.

definition: Covering or including everything without exception; total in scope or extent.
example: The treaty imposed an all-encompassing trade embargo that affected every sector of the country's economy.
mnemonic: ALL-ENCOMPASSING: wraps its arms around ALL things — nothing left outside.

definition: Present in large amounts; abundant and plentiful.
example: She took copious notes during the lecture, filling three notebooks with detailed observations.
mnemonic: COPIOUS: Latin 'copia' = plenty — think a cornucopia overflowing with food.

definition: Familiar with and at ease in many different cultures and countries; broadly inclusive of diverse elements.
example: New York's cosmopolitan population brings together communities from virtually every nation on earth.
mnemonic: COSMOPOLITAN: 'cosmos' (world) + 'polis' (city) — a citizen of the whole world.

definition: A scarcity or inadequate supply of something; a notable lack.
example: The dearth of qualified candidates forced the committee to extend the application deadline by two months.
mnemonic: DEARTH: sounds like 'death' — when something is so scarce it has nearly died out.

definition: Extremely small in size; tiny.
example: The diminutive sparrow perched on the windowsill, barely large enough to be visible from across the room.
mnemonic: DIMINUTIVE: DIMINished to the smallest size — diminished all the way.

definition: Deriving ideas or styles from a broad and diverse range of sources rather than one tradition.
example: Her eclectic taste in music ranged from Renaissance madrigals to modern electronic compositions.
mnemonic: ECLECTIC: 'ek-legein' (Greek: pick out) — selecting the best from many different sources.

definition: Thorough and complete to the fullest extent; leaving nothing out.
example: The commission conducted an exhaustive review of every document before reaching its verdict.
mnemonic: EXHAUSTIVE: EXHAUSTs every possibility — covers everything until nothing remains.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'exhausted' (tired); exhaustive means thorough and complete, not fatigued.

definition: Irrelevant or unrelated to the subject at hand; not essential.
example: The editor cut all extraneous details from the report, leaving only data directly relevant to the findings.
mnemonic: EXTRANEOUS: EXTRA things that don't belong — tossed in from outside the subject.
confusableNote: Do not confuse with 'extrinsic' (coming from outside); extraneous specifically means irrelevant or unnecessary.

definition: Highly productive or fertile, whether in generating offspring, ideas, or creative output.
example: The fecund novelist published seven books in a single decade, each exploring a different literary form.
mnemonic: FECUND: sounds like 'fecundity' — the rich soil of fertility producing abundantly.

definition: The outer or less important margin of a group, activity, or subject; a peripheral or extreme position.
example: The theory remained on the fringe of academic debate, rejected by most mainstream scholars.
mnemonic: FRINGE: the decorative edge of a rug — far from the center, easily overlooked.

definition: Producing good or abundant results; highly productive or beneficial.
example: The partnership proved fruitful, yielding three joint publications in the first year alone.
mnemonic: FRUITFUL: a tree that bears much FRUIT — effort that yields rich results.

definition: Composed of elements of different kinds; diverse and varied in composition.
example: The city's heterogeneous population made it a rich cultural mosaic of languages and traditions.
mnemonic: HETEROGENEOUS: 'hetero' (different) + 'genus' (kind) — many different kinds mixed together.
confusableNote: Directly contrasts with 'homogeneous' (all the same kind).

definition: Of the same kind throughout; uniform in composition or character.
example: The homogeneous community had shared the same customs and language for generations without outside influence.
mnemonic: HOMOGENEOUS: 'homo' (same) + 'genus' (kind) — all one kind, like homogenized milk.

definition: Occurring as a minor accompaniment to something else; not of primary importance.
example: The cost savings were incidental benefits of the policy, not its primary motivation.
mnemonic: INCIDENTAL: an INCIDENT on the side — happens alongside the main event, not central to it.

definition: Exceeding reasonable limits; disproportionately large or excessive.
example: He spent an inordinate amount of time on the introduction, leaving little for the body of the argument.
mnemonic: INORDINATE: IN- + ordinate (ordered) — out of order, way beyond what's appropriate.

definition: To treat a person or group as insignificant or peripheral, relegating them to powerless status.
example: The policy effectively marginalized rural communities by concentrating all funding in urban centers.
mnemonic: MARGINALIZE: push to the MARGIN — written off to the side where no one reads.

definition: Lacking in quantity or quality; deficient and inadequate.
example: The refugees survived on meager rations of rice and water for weeks before aid arrived.
mnemonic: MEAGER: sounds like 'meager meal' — barely enough to sustain you.

definition: Extremely small in size or amount; tiny.
example: The forensic analyst found minute traces of the substance invisible to the naked eye.
mnemonic: MINUTE (adj): pronounced my-NEWT — shrunk down to a newt's scale, almost nothing.
confusableNote: As an adjective (my-NOOT), minute means tiny — distinct from the noun 'minute' (60 seconds).

definition: Having many different aspects, features, or sides; complex and many-dimensional.
example: The multifaceted crisis required coordinated responses from economists, public health officials, and diplomats.
mnemonic: MULTIFACETED: MULTI- + FACETS — like a gemstone with many cut faces shining in different directions.

definition: Present everywhere or in all places at the same time; ubiquitous.
example: Social media has become omnipresent, shaping public discourse in nearly every corner of the world.
mnemonic: OMNIPRESENT: OMNI (all) + PRESENT — showing up everywhere, all the time.

definition: Miserably small or inadequate in amount; contemptibly trivial.
example: Workers protested the paltry raise of two cents per hour as an insult given the company's record profits.
mnemonic: PALTRY: PALtry — pale and thin, so small it barely exists.

definition: Constituting only a part; incomplete or not total.
example: The committee reached only a partial agreement, leaving several contentious issues unresolved.
mnemonic: PARTIAL: only PART of the whole — the rest is still missing.
confusableNote: Note 'partial' also means biased (favoring one side), so context determines which sense applies on the GRE.
